text: I'm Dr. Karen Hildebrand with AWS.
And I'm Travis Mellinger with Cisco, and this is my architecture.
So Travis, Cisco is a large organization.
You brought us an architecture.
Can you tell us what this particular architecture sells for?
Yes, so we are talking about the Large Language Model Proxy,
which is an application that runs here in our EKS cloud.
And what the LLM proxy does is make it easier for developers working on the WebEx portfolio of applications
to integrate large language models into the experiences they're trying to build into the WebEx portfolio.
So you've abstracted a bit of the complexity of using large language models.
Can you tell us how you did that?
Sure.
So it is a relatively simple service that runs here in the cloud that provides
some useful abstractions and common patterns that developers working on WebEx applications are
accustomed to. So one example is identity, authentication, authorization. There is a
common framework for that inside the WebEx ecosystem, which the Large Language Model Proxy adheres to,
like the rest of the applications. So developers, when they want to talk to it, they can talk to it in a
secure way that looks just like all the other applications. And it also gives us a nice way to
collect information about large language model providers. If we want to know, you know, when
somebody is making requests to Bedrock or to SageMaker or all the way out here to an external provider. We
have teams that are trying all sorts of different providers and models. We have one place where we can
capture information, operational information, and also some data where it's appropriate. We can do that in
one place instead of your developers all the way over here having to do that in number of times for
each one of these providers and models they want to integrate.
So say I'm an N developer and I want to submit a request for Titan response.
Right.
How would that flow through?
Simple. You make a request to our team. We would like support for Titan. We already have support for Titan.
It's already in there. But if they have a model that they would like to use, then we plug it in, if you will,
to the Large Language Model Proxy. And then the only thing that they need to know to make a request from
their service over here is what the shape of the request looks like. Obviously, Bedrock looks a little bit
different from SageMaker. It looks a little bit different from some other providers API, but that's all they need to
know. Instead of having to do the full API integration, they simply need to know what the shape of the input
looks like and then they're on their way.
Terrific. And then as that is captured, can you tell me a little bit about how you think about that
from a regulatory future proofing the design and potentially a data lake structure?
Sure. So you mentioned our data lake, which is all of this bit down here, and it solves a number of problems.
So the ones you mentioned, kind of future looking regulatory sort of things. We have a responsibility
and we have a responsible AI framework that helps us govern how we think about this. But we have
a responsibility to make sure that the platform, that the large language models that we're integrating,
they're not being abused. So we need to prevent abuse. We need to prevent security issues. We need to
prevent prompt injections, among many other issues that could come up. We have a responsibility to make sure that
we have solved those kinds of problems. But then we also, we have these operational concerns where we
want to understand how models are performing. We want to understand how much usage we have of a given
model. So an application making a request to the LLM proxy, which then, you know, say goes over here again
to bedrock, we know who made that request. We know how many requests they made. We're logging, we're recording
metrics for all of this information so that we can then use this over here to query it, which helps us with
problems like cost optimization. You know, if we want to know, you know, if we have, you know,
particularly high usage of one model, that might give us a good reason to go to provisioned pricing
instead of on-demand pricing. All that operational information helps us make decisions like this.
And also probably helps with things like latency and understanding response times based on what model
was selected, things like that. Exactly. All of those sorts of things. Like all of that, we can gain those
insights by the information we capture right here. But it's not just the operational information. We also,
you know, we want to be able to train better models. We want to be able to validate how well
our models are behaving. But we also have a responsibility to our customers to ensure that
we are using data in ways that they expect us to use, the way they have consented to use.
Large language model proxy solves that problem for us very well. So where customers have indicated
consent to us, you know, whether they're, you know, they're working on us in part, working with us in
partnership on a new feature, or if they're participating in trials and consent to allow
us to use data, whatever the reason may be, one of these applications over here in the ecosystem
simply needs to indicate consent on the customer's behalf to the proxy. And then we can capture this
information in one place. Whereas if we were solving the same problem over here in a WebEx microservice,
depending on which provider they plugged in, like they would have to do all of that over here. They would have to
capture the information, they would have to store it in a secure way. Instead, we do it all in one spot.
That's fantastic. So you've basically done the undifferentiated heavy lifting for your application
developers. Yes, I only learned that term recently, and I love it because that is exactly what we're doing.
Fantastic. Thanks for taking the time to share this architecture with us today, Travis. This is really
exciting, and I think there will be a lot of customers who will benefit from hearing from this.
Of course. Thank you very much for having me.
Thank you.
